html: ⚠ Figure failed to load

elements: multiple identical camera models (compact cuboid body with a short frustum lens, colored consistently, arrayed evenly along the X axis)
solid colored arrows anchored at each camera, pointing along the X axis to indicate motion direction and magnitude (red for right, blue for left)
semi-transparent horizontal ground plane beneath the cameras for depth grounding
stationary background scene: stylized objects at different distances along Z (a 'cloud' far away, a mid-depth post or tree, a foreground marker/rock), each represented by simple geometric shapes
dashed projection lines from the far/near objects onto aligned image planes of each camera (optional, but clarifies optical flow relationship)

interactions.label: Lateral speed (Vx)
interactions.teaches: Shows how changing the camera's lateral velocity alters the direction and size of the motion arrows, and thus the observed motion in the scene.

interactions.label: Near object depth
interactions.teaches: Adjusts the Z position of the nearest object, demonstrating how the apparent motion of objects at different depths changes: closer objects move faster across the image plane.

interactions.label: Show far object
interactions.teaches: Toggles visibility of a distant object (like a cloud), making it easy to see that distant objects barely move under lateral translation.

demo_steps.title: No Camera Motion
demo_steps.narration: With the lateral speed set to zero, you see a row of stationary cameras and objects at different depths. Notice there are no arrows—the camera is not moving, and the scene remains static from every viewpoint.

demo_steps.title: Moderate Rightward Motion
demo_steps.narration: Let's introduce rightward lateral motion: the arrows now point right and their length shows the speed. Watch how the nearby object appears to move quickly across the image plane, while the far object (the cloud) barely shifts, illustrating the parallax effect.

demo_steps.title: Increase Depth Difference
demo_steps.narration: Now we'll move the near object further away. Notice how its motion across the camera's image planes becomes slower—closer and farther objects show a big difference in their apparent speeds, while the distant cloud remains almost motionless.

demo_steps.title: Hide Distant Reference
demo_steps.narration: Finally, let's hide the far object. The near object's motion is still visible, but it's harder to appreciate the contrast in motion across depths without a distant point for reference. Comparing these cases helps reveal why parallax is such a strong cue for depth.

plan.camera_suggestion: Start with a slight overhead and three-quarters front/right view, so the X-axis (motion direction) is clear, all cameras/arrows are visible, and the depth (Z) between scene objects and cameras is apparent. Zoom to fit all geometry, including the farthest object, with some empty space for context.
plan.notes: Use consistent coloring for cameras and arrowheads; arrows should scale in length (and flip direction) based on 'lateralSpeed'. Distant objects should be visually less saturated to imply depth. When 'showFarObject' is false, smoothly fade out the far object. Overlay (optional) motion trail lines for objects moving across the image plane, to clarify projected optical flow if technically feasible. Anchor camera frustums to the ground plane for spatial clarity.

elements: five identical simplified camera frustums (boxes with truncated pyramids and a lens dot at tip)
solid red arrows indicating direction and magnitude of lateral camera motion
horizontal ground plane (implicit from layout, optional for 3D grounding)
even spacing between cameras along horizontal (X) axis

interactions.label: Lateral speed (Vx)
interactions.teaches: Adjusts the lateral (sideways) velocity of the camera, making the translation arrows longer or shorter and changing their direction.

interactions.label: Number of Cameras
interactions.teaches: Sets the number of cameras shown in the sequence, letting users visualize the sampled path in more or fewer steps.

interactions.label: Arrow Opacity
interactions.teaches: Changes how visible the motion arrows are, emphasizing or de-emphasizing the direction of motion.

demo_steps.title: Start with Zero Motion
demo_steps.narration: Here's a row of cameras, but notice there are no movement arrows because the lateral speed is zero. The scene is completely still — there's no sideways motion to observe.

demo_steps.title: Increase Lateral Motion
demo_steps.narration: Now let's move the camera to the right by increasing the lateral speed. See how the arrows appear and point right, showing the direction of camera motion. If you adjust the speed, the arrows grow longer for faster movement, or shorter for slower movement.

demo_steps.title: Reverse and Compare
demo_steps.narration: Let's try a negative lateral speed: now the arrows point left, indicating the camera moves the other way. You can also reduce the number of cameras to focus on a smaller sequence, or change arrow opacity to see the effect more clearly. Adjusting these controls helps you explore different lateral motion scenarios.

interactionPlan.camera_suggestion: Position the viewpoint above and slightly in front of the horizontal camera sequence, looking down at a shallow angle so all cameras and arrows are clearly visible and the sense of right/left motion is obvious.
interactionPlan.notes: Ensure arrows scale accurately with the lateralSpeed value (both length and direction) and remain visually distinct regardless of camera count or opacity setting. Use simple geometric camera icons for clarity. Consider soft shadows or subtle ground reflections for geometric grounding, but keep the focus on the directionality and count of camera elements.

attempts.html: ⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: The original figure shows 6 camera icons, while the default generated scene shows only 5.
Camera icons in the original are evenly spaced apart; spacing in the generated figure may vary based on slider but does not match original exactly at default.
The original figure has no labels, but the generated figure includes a top label and a velocity label.
Control and UI elements in the generated figure are not present in the original.
Original arrows are very thick and absent of transparency; arrow thickness and color match closely, though slight differences in length and taper exist.
evaluation.notes: Excellent interactivity and diagram construction; default count/spatial layout mismatch the original, and excessive labeling slightly detracts from faithfulness.
action_items: Set the default camera position count to 6 to exactly match the source figure.
Adjust default camera icon spacing to more closely match the proportions of the original figure.
Provide an option to hide all non-source labels for a purer textbook style.
Ensure arrows' length and head proportions more closely match the original schematic.
Review arrow opacity defaults for better visual match to the solid fills in the original.

feedback.rationale: All action items and discrepancies focus on styling, default parameters, and minor visual adjustments—indicating the concept and structure are correct but the generator needs tuning for exact fidelity.
